driver.body: Density is not bought; it is compounded [S-008]. The fleet is the physical instrument that converts capital into interactions, and the discipline is to concentrate that instrument until the data it produces crosses a usefulness threshold — then hold it there while demand catches up [S-008]. This driver owns **D**, the data term of the moat identity M = D × B × L × T: inside an already-dense zone every additional interaction sharpens the routing and pricing models rather than merely adding revenue, which is why concentration, not coverage, is the governing rule and why the two mechanisms below both bend toward holding a saturated core rather than spreading a thin blanket [S-009].

mechanisms.body:
Kepler does not spread vehicles to maximise city coverage; it floods zones until interaction density per km² per hour crosses the threshold where the routing and pricing models stop guessing [S-009]. **Below roughly 40 interactions/km²/hr the models are extrapolating; above it they are interpolating**, and every downstream prediction — ETA, surge, charger dwell — sharpens. The mechanism moves D directly: rather than 300 vehicles thin across a metro, Kepler runs 1,100 inside three contiguous zones [S-009]. The trade-off is brutal and deliberate — visible under-service at the metro edge, and a press narrative of "Kepler abandoned the suburbs," accepted because a dense core produces data that a thin blanket never will [S-101].

The faceted map renders the rule as a metro canvas of named zones, each labelled with active vehicles and utilization (revenue-minutes ÷ available-minutes). Its decisive contrast is **Marina Core at 71% against Noord Fringe at 39%**: the same vehicle earns and learns nearly twice as much inside the saturated zone [S-102]. Noord Fringe is the edge-case the rule tolerates — a starving polygon kept deliberately thin so capital flows to Marina Core [S-102]. Saturated zones are outlined solid; sub-threshold zones are hatched, a standing to-do list of where density has not yet been bought. The Reforma Corridor sits between the two states, ramping toward the 40-interaction floor: it is where the rule is being executed rather than defended, and its trajectory over the next three quarters is the leading indicator of whether the density-first thesis generalises beyond Singapore and Amsterdam's founding geometry [S-009].

Concentration only compounds if utilization stays high enough that the fleet is interacting rather than idling — an idle AV produces no D and burns the depreciation that Driver 4 quantifies [S-010]. Kepler manages the fleet against a **utilization floor**, dispatching repositioning moves and dynamic pricing — fed by D4's cash-free rails — whenever a zone threatens to slip beneath it. The mechanism moves D by protecting the denominator of interactions-per-vehicle; the number it moves is weekday utilization, **held above the 60% floor in saturated zones** [S-010].

The utilization time-series for Marina Core plots a 24-hour curve, midnight to midnight, against a dashed 60% floor line. Two peaks — 07:30 and 18:15 — clear 80%, while the 14:00 midday trough kisses the floor and triggers repositioning and a fare cut [S-103]. The edge-case is the trough that breaks the floor: a rain-driven demand collapse on a Tuesday dropped Reforma Corridor to 41% for two hours, the signal that a zone is over-vehicled and capital should rotate out [S-104]. The chain to Driver 2 is direct — a fleet held at the density floor generates enough incident-free exposure that safety becomes statistically legible, which is the only way to earn the trust the next driver depends on [S-010].
